run_name: NATP Module 15: Observation and Charting — Source-First Dry Run
purpose: The purpose of this unit is to prepare students to know how, when, and why to use objective and subjective observation skills. They will report and record observations on appropriate documents using medical terms and abbreviations.

objectives.objective_title: Define observation and list the senses used to observe a patient/resident.
objectives.exact_objective_statement: Define observation and list the senses used to observe a patient/resident.
objectives.source_excerpt: Define observation and list the senses used to observe a patient/resident. A. Observation � use of senses to collect information about a patient/resident 1. Senses used for evaluation a. Sight b. Touch c. Hearing d. Smell 2. Observations should be made with regard to a patient's/resident's a. Skin color and temperature b. Mood and mental status c. Behavior, movement d. Unusual odors e. Respiration f. Ability to respond g. Appetite h. Performance of ADL i. Elimination j. Pain or discomfort Learn to observe the patient/resident throughout daily contacts noting any changes or needs 4. Report all changes or needs to licensed nurse Module 15: Observation and Charting B. ABCs of observation A. Lecture/Discussion A. Written test 1. Appearance B. Develop lists of B. Class participation 2. Behavior 3. Communication observable signs and A. Written test statements from client B. Class participation

objectives.objective_title: List types of charting documents and the use for each
objectives.exact_objective_statement: List types of charting documents and the use for each
objectives.source_excerpt: E. Example of Patient care List types of charting documents and the uses for each. A. Patient/resident record and patient's/resident's chart summary/care plan 1. Communicates and records health history, status, and treatment 2. A legal record B. Patient care summary/care plan 1. Summarizes physician's orders 2. Identifies critical data such as allergies, code status, diet, activity, etc. 3. Gives medication and treatment information C. Nursing care plan 1. Lists patient's/resident's need and provides specific nursing activities that address the patient's/resident's needs 2. A guide for the Nurse Assistant for delivering care D. Graphic sheet - used in some facilities to document Module 15: Observation and Charting 1. Vital signs A. Lecture/Discussion A. Written test 2. Intake and output B. Refer to MDS form B. Class participation C. Document 3. Weight Section G Appendix E. ADL sheet 7.5. ONLINE FORM patient/resident ADL C. Refer to Appendix H- functioning using the 1. Used to document care at each shift for activities of daily living MDS Version 3.0 All ADL flow sheet 2. The record on which most facilities have the care work chart Forms Access form at

objectives.objective_title: Explain how to accurately complete ADL assessment for MDS
objectives.exact_objective_statement: Explain how to accurately complete ADL assessment for MDS
objectives.source_excerpt: http://www.cms.hhs.gov/nu Explain how to accurately complete ADL assessment for MDS. rsinghomequalityinits/ A. Minimum Data Set (MDS) 1. Mandated assessment tool by Federal regulation (483.20 (b)) and (F272) to give facility structured, standardized approach to care 2. Basis for payment/or reimbursement to the facility B. Patient/resident Assessment Instrument (RAI) 1. Each facility must use its state-specified RAI to assess newly admitted patients/residents, conduct an annual reassessment and assess those patients/residents who experience a significant change in status 2. The facility is responsible for addressing all needs and strengths of patients/residents regardless of whether the issue is included in the Minimum Data Set (MDS) or Patient/resident Assessment Protocol (RAPs) 3. The scope of the RAI does not limit the facility's responsibility to assess and address all care needed by the patient/resident C. MDS Section G refers to physical functioning and structural problems 1. Bathing 2. Functional limitation in Range of Motion 3. Modes of Transfer D. Assessment Reference Day (ARD) 1. ARD � set date for the last day of assessment period 2. Assessments are made during the seven days preceding the ARD (the look back period) 3. Example: ARD is Jan. 8. Therefore, the look back period of observation will start from Jan. 2 until Jan. 8 Module 15: Observation and Charting E. Two categories of ADL section: 1. Patient/resident ADL Self-performance - what the patient/resident actually did for himself or herself and/or how much verbal or physical help was required by staff members
objectives.weight_reason: Deterministic source-density allocation from source minimum theory hours; assessment/clinical/certificate time excluded.
content_outline: Source objective statement: Explain how to accurately complete ADL assessment for MDS

objectives.objective_title: Discuss procedures to use when recording on a patient's/resident's chart
objectives.exact_objective_statement: Discuss procedures to use when recording on a patient's/resident's chart
objectives.source_excerpt: Discuss procedures used when recording on a patient's/resident's chart. A. Charting procedures 1. Make sure you have the correct chart or ADL sheet 2. Write legibly and neatly a. Write notes on paper first b. Check for accuracy and spelling 3. Place events in proper sequence 4. Chart according to facility/policy standards 5. Be concise and use appropriate terms and abbreviations 6. Always use ballpoint pen a. Black ink b. No felt tip, fountain pens, pencils or gel pens c. Use color only if approved in facility policy 7. Errors � cross out with one line a. DO NOT ERASE OR USE WHITEOUT b. Write "error" above the line c. Initial the entry 8. Include patient's/resident's complete information on each page a. Some facilities have imprint stampers b. If not, write in patient's/resident's name and any other information as the facility mandates 9. Never skip lines 10. Signature example; J. Jones, Nurse Assistant or R. Smith, NA. Module 15: Observation and Charting 11. Always date and time entries � or make sure you are charting under correct date and time 12. Chart only procedures YOU HAVE PERFORMED � and only after you have performed the procedure 13. Never chart for someone else 14. Chart only those observations which you know to be true (objective data) a. Do not chart opinions b. Subjective data must be in quotation marks and exactly as the patient/resident states B. Computers and charting 1. Some facilities may have a computerized charting system 2. Basic principles of charting apply � confidentiality and privacy are important issues 3. Systems are password protected a. Each us
